title: Client Scopes and Protocol Mappers
description:
## Adding a Groups Claim to the Access Token

An API needs to authorize based on the user's **group membership**, but the access tokens Keycloak issues currently contain **no `groups` claim**. How do you get the user's groups into the token?

options.text: Add a **group-membership protocol mapper** (typically via a client scope) so the mapper injects a `groups` claim into the token.
Edit each user's profile and paste their group names into a custom attribute by hand.
Change a **realm setting** to make all claims appear in every token automatically.
Lengthen the access-token lifespan so it has room for more claims.

explanation: **Protocol mappers** are what put claims into tokens. To surface group membership you add a **Group Membership mapper**, usually packaged inside a **client scope** so it's reusable and can be assigned to clients as a default or optional scope. Optional scopes are pulled in by passing them in the OIDC `scope` parameter; default scopes are always applied. Option **b** is brittle manual work that doesn't reflect real-time membership and isn't how claims are produced. Option **c** doesn't exist — there is no realm switch that dumps every claim into every token; claims are controlled by mappers/scopes. Option **d** confuses token *lifetime* with token *contents*; lifespan has nothing to do with which claims are present.

hints.title: Mappers shape tokens
hints.body: Whenever a claim is missing from a token, the answer is almost always 'configure a protocol mapper' — and grouping mappers into a client scope makes them reusable.
